behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from recommendation results regardless of relevance score
behaviors.justification: This tests a filter that must work across the full recommendation pipeline. A Unit test with mocked inventory data could miss failures where the filter isn't applied at the right point in the system flow, or where product metadata isn't properly propagated. Integration testing with a real test database containing stock status ensures the filter correctly integrates with product data retrieval.
behaviors.test_description: Load a test database with 50 products (30 in-stock, 20 out-of-stock) with varying relevance scores. Generate recommendations for a test user. Assert that no out-of-stock products appear in results, even if they have high relevance scores.

behaviors.description: User preference filter correctly excludes product categories in the user's blocklist
behaviors.justification: User preferences are persisted data that must flow through the recommendation pipeline. Unit tests with mocked preferences might miss issues with how blocked categories are matched against product categories, or where the filter isn't applied to all results. Integration testing with a real user profile in a test database catches category matching and filter application failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test user with blocked categories ['Electronics', 'Sports']. Load a test database with 40 products across 6 categories. Generate recommendations and assert that no products from blocked categories appear in results.

behaviors.description: Recommendation algorithm ranks products matching recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal
behaviors.justification: The ranking algorithm needs to integrate with real browse history data. Unit tests can verify the ranking formula in isolation, but Integration tests are needed to ensure browse history is correctly retrieved, matched to products, and used in the ranking calculation. The failure would be: browse history exists but isn't factored into ranking.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test user with browse history: 5 visits to Product A, 3 visits to similar Product B, 0 visits to dissimilar Product C. Generate recommendations. Assert that Product A and B rank higher than Product C when all have similar base popularity scores.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns correct page of results with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if underlying data hasn't changed
behaviors.justification: Pagination bugs (offset/limit errors, non-deterministic sort order) typically hide in real database retrieval. A Unit test on offset logic might pass, but Integration testing reveals if the sort order is properly specified to the database, if result ordering is stable across calls, and if pagination boundaries are correct with real data.
behaviors.test_description: Populate test database with 50 products. Request page 2 (items 11-20) twice with identical parameters. Assert both requests return the same product IDs in the same order. Verify all 5 items on the page match expected range.

behaviors.description: Recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement)
behaviors.justification: Serendipity logic depends on comparing user's browse history against all product categories. Unit tests of the serendipity selector in isolation miss failures where browse history isn't properly fetched, or where category matching is wrong. Integration testing with realistic user history and product catalog ensures the serendipity logic correctly identifies unbrowsed categories.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with browse history only in 'Books' and 'Music' categories. Load 40 products across 6 categories. Generate 30 recommendations (top results). Assert that at least 1 product from 'Electronics', 'Sports', 'Home', or 'Fashion' (unbrowsed categories) appears in results.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set
behaviors.justification: The fallback path (popularity-based ranking for new users) must integrate with the popularity data source and recommendation pipeline. Unit tests of the fallback logic alone miss failures where popularity data isn't available, isn't properly joined with products, or isn't used when user history is empty. Integration testing ensures the fallback is triggered and returns valid results.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new user with no purchase or browse history. Load test database with 40 products with pre-computed popularity scores. Generate recommendations. Assert that result set is non-empty and contains products, ranked by popularity score, not by user-specific signals.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with
behaviors.justification: Click tracking involves writing data to persistent storage and must be verified against the actual storage layer. A Unit test mocking the storage layer would pass even if data isn't actually persisted or if columns are mapped incorrectly. Integration testing with a real test database ensures the click record is written with correct fields and can be retrieved.
behaviors.test_description: Generate recommendations for a user, simulate 3 clicks (on items at positions 1, 5, and 12). Query the test database for click records. Assert that all 3 clicks are stored with correct product IDs, positions, timestamps, and user ID.

behaviors.description: Response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance testing requires realistic data scale and structures to reveal algorithmic inefficiencies. Unit tests on small datasets hide O(n²) complexity bugs. Integration testing with 100k products in a test database reveals if filtering, ranking, and pagination scale efficiently. System level would be needed only if production database has different performance characteristics.
behaviors.test_description: Populate test database with 100,000 products with realistic distribution of stock status, categories, and popularity scores. Create test user with browse history. Measure time to generate top 20 recommendations (including filtering, ranking, pagination). Assert response time is consistently under 200ms across 10 runs.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: Deprioritization logic depends on accessing user's purchase history and applying it in the ranking function. Unit tests of the deprioritization penalty in isolation miss failures where purchase history isn't fetched, or where the penalty isn't correctly applied to ranking. Integration testing with realistic purchase history ensures the algorithm correctly reads and uses purchase data.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with browse history. Generate initial recommendations, note top 10 products. Mark 2 products as purchased. Generate recommendations again. Assert that previously purchased products rank lower in second request, with at least some products from initial top 10 no longer in top 10 of second request.

behaviors.description: Recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list
behaviors.justification: Determinism failures come from non-deterministic operations (random sampling, unsorted results, floating-point precision) that appear when testing with real data at scale. Unit tests on small inputs might miss non-determinism. Integration testing with multiple identical requests and realistic dataset reveals if sorting is properly specified or if random operations exist.
behaviors.test_description: Freeze time and user state. Generate recommendations for a user, capture full ranked list with scores. Generate recommendations again with no intervening changes. Assert that both results contain identical product IDs in identical order. Repeat 5 times to confirm consistency.
